summary: Rayan is known for safely migrating and operating high-volume payments and fraud infrastructure (zero-downtime Stripe migration, strict p99 targets, and Q4 holiday resilience) while writing the alignment artifacts and runbooks that make large cross-team changes possible. Their work emphasizes operational safety (idempotency, performance tuning, migrations), clear documentation, and mentorship through thorough reviews and pairing, with a track record of stepping in to unblock cross-team deliverables.

skills.name: Distributed systems migration and reliability
skills.summary: Zero-downtime migrations and holiday-peak reliability for $2M/day payment infrastructure.
skills.description: Rayan has repeatedly demonstrated the ability to safely migrate and operate high-stakes distributed systems under production traffic. The Stripe Payment Intents migration (PR #1283) used a dual-write, shadow-compare, and feature-flagged rollback strategy to cut over $2M/day in transactions across 14 markets with zero downtime. During Q4 holiday peak (FRAUD-44), he owned end-to-end reliability of the fraud-scoring system, achieving 0 P1 incidents and resolving the single P2 in 22 minutes. These aren't isolated wins — they reflect a consistent pattern of operational discipline, careful rollout planning, and the ability to keep critical-path systems stable while their foundations are being changed.
evidence: PR #1283 — Led zero-downtime Stripe Payment Intents migration with dual-write and shadow-compare for $2M/day traffic across 14 markets.
FRAUD-44 — Owned Q4 holiday reliability for fraud-scoring system; 0 P1s, 1 P2 resolved in 22 min, and no customer-visible outages.

skills.name: Database performance and schema evolution
skills.summary: Query optimization, index tuning, and partitioning strategy for high-write fraud infrastructure.
skills.description: Rayan's database work targets the specific performance bottlenecks of the fraud-scoring path. PR #1340 reduced fraud-rules query latency by 40% at p99 through composite indexes, a targeted ANALYZE cadence, and removal of a write-heavy unused trigger — a change that required understanding both the query patterns and the write amplification characteristics of the existing schema. FRAUD-127 planned monthly partitioning to bound index size and keep hot read paths fast as transaction volume grows. This is hands-on, measured optimization work grounded in production query profiles, not speculative tuning.
evidence: PR #1340 — Reduced fraud-rules query latency 40% at p99 via composite indexes, targeted ANALYZE cadence, and removal of write-heavy triggers.
FRAUD-127 — Planned monthly partitioning to bound index size and keep hot read paths fast.

skills.name: Technical writing and design alignment
skills.summary: RFCs, API docs, and runbooks that coordinate multi-team infrastructure changes.
skills.description: Rayan authored the alignment artifacts that made large cross-team changes possible. RFC 0011 (Stripe migration) and RFC 0014 (idempotency design) were used by Risk, Ledger, SRE, and Fraud Platform to coordinate their respective changes — these aren't docs that sat unread, they were active coordination tools referenced in design reviews. He also wrote or co-authored the canonical fraud-scoring API documentation and the incident runbook that on-call engineers use during P1 triage. The consistent pattern is writing that reduces coordination costs and makes it possible for other teams to integrate safely without synchronous meetings.
evidence: RFC 0011 and RFC 0014 — Authored alignment artifacts for Stripe migration and idempotency design used across Risk, Ledger, SRE, and Fraud Platform.
docs/api/fraud-scoring.md and docs/runbooks/fraud-scoring-incident.md — Primary author/co-author of canonical API docs and on-call runbooks.

skills.name: Internal developer tooling and portals
skills.summary: Contributed to the Developer Portal to surface service ownership and reduce incident triage time.
skills.description: Rayan added Fraud Platform services to the internal Developer Portal (DEVPORTAL-22) and integrated the service catalog (PR #1456), making service owners and dependency graphs discoverable in one place. This work reduced incident triage time by eliminating the need to Slack-search for 'who owns this service?' during P1s. While not his primary focus area, it shows willingness to invest in cross-team infrastructure that benefits the broader engineering org.
evidence: DEVPORTAL-22 — Added Fraud Platform services to internal Developer Portal, improving discoverability of owners and dependencies.
github:pr-1456 — Integrated service catalog into portal to reduce incident triage time.

skills.name: Code review depth and mentorship
skills.summary: Detailed reviews covering correctness, performance, and migration safety, plus active pairing and mentoring.
skills.description: Across multiple review cycles (reviews 2089, 1322, 1389), Rayan provided targeted feedback on race conditions, error-handling edge cases, and migration safety — often pairing with the PR author rather than just leaving comments. In Slack threads he repeatedly unblocked teammates on cold-start troubleshooting and walked newer contributors through design decisions. This is mentorship that shows up in others' velocity improvements, not in Rayan's own commit count — the kind of invisible work that's easy to miss but high-leverage for the team.
evidence: github:review-2089, github:review-1322, github:review-1389 — Provided detailed review feedback and pairing to address race conditions, error-handling, and migration safety.
slack-threads.md — Repeated mentoring interactions, including cold-start troubleshooting for teammates and design walkthroughs for newer contributors.

projectThemes.title: Payments & Fraud Platform Reliability
projectThemes.summary: High-volume payments and fraud infrastructure with strict p99 targets, zero-downtime migrations, and holiday resilience.
projectThemes.description: Rayan led the zero-downtime migration from the legacy payment processor to Stripe Payment Intents (PR #1283), handling $2M/day in transaction volume across 14 markets using a dual-write, shadow-compare, and feature-flagged rollback strategy. He then reduced fraud-rules query latency by 40% at p99 (PR #1340) through composite indexes, a targeted ANALYZE cadence, and removal of a write-heavy unused trigger. During Q4 holiday peak (FRAUD-44), he owned end-to-end reliability of the fraud-scoring system — resulting in zero P1 incidents and a single P2 resolved in 22 minutes with no customer-visible outages. Collectively these efforts kept the fraud-scoring path under its 50ms p99 budget while the underlying infrastructure was being actively modernized.

projectThemes.title: Operational Safety & Developer Experience
projectThemes.summary: Safety mechanisms — idempotency, runbooks, API contracts — that reduce operational risk for downstream teams.
projectThemes.description: Rayan authored RFC 0014, the idempotency design spec used by Risk, Ledger, SRE, and Fraud Platform to coordinate replay-safe request handling across services. He also wrote the canonical fraud-scoring API documentation (docs/api/fraud-scoring.md) and the incident runbook (docs/runbooks/fraud-scoring-incident.md) that on-call engineers reference during P1 triage. PR #1401 implemented the idempotency-key contract in the webhook router, adding replay protection that prevents duplicate charges during payment-processor retries. These artifacts collectively lowered the bar for other teams integrating with the fraud-scoring path and reduced mean-time-to-resolve for production incidents by giving responders a single source of truth.

projectThemes.title: Mentorship & Cross-team Support
projectThemes.summary: Deep code reviews, pairing on migrations, and portal work that makes system ownership discoverable.
projectThemes.description: Rayan provided detailed review feedback across multiple PRs (reviews 2089, 1322, 1389) covering race conditions, error-handling edge cases, and migration safety — often pairing with the author to walk through fixes rather than just leaving comments. He championed adding Fraud Platform services to the internal Developer Portal (DEVPORTAL-22, PR #1456), reducing incident triage time by making service owners and dependency graphs discoverable in one place. In Slack threads he repeatedly unblocked teammates on cold-start troubleshooting and walked newer contributors through design decisions. This pattern of investment in others' velocity and organizational knowledge is consistent across multiple quarters of artifacts.

contributionEvidence.summary: PR #1283 — Led zero-downtime migration of legacy payment processor to Stripe Payment Intents for $2M/day across 14 markets with dual-write, shadow-compare, and feature-flagged rollback.
FRAUD-44 — Owned end-to-end reliability of the fraud-scoring system through Q4 holiday peak; 0 P1s, 1 P2 (resolved in 22 min), and zero customer-visible outages.
RFC 0011 — Authored migration plan and alignment artifact used by Risk, Ledger, SRE, and Fraud Platform to coordinate the Stripe Payment Intents migration.
PR #1340 — Reduced fraud-rules query latency 40% at p99 via composite indexes, targeted ANALYZE cadence, and removal of a write-heavy unused trigger.
Multiple GitHub reviews (PRs 1267, 1322, 489, 1389) — Provided targeted feedback on correctness, performance impact, and migration safety while mentoring newer contributors.
DEVPORTAL-22 — Cross-team contribution to surface Fraud Platform services in the internal Developer Portal, unblocking Q1 launch and reducing incident triage time.

summary: Tisha is a frontend platform engineer who demonstrates strong evidence of modernizing UI architecture (React Server Components, design tokens) and raising security baselines across web surfaces (strict CSP, SRI, cookie hardening, XSS audits). She also takes cross-team ownership, performing database migrations and coordinating security reviews while producing runbooks and mentoring peers on safe patterns.

skills.name: Frontend Architecture (React, Server Components, streaming)
skills.summary: RSC migration with streaming that cut TTI from 2.4s to 0.9s, plus token-driven design system expansion.
skills.description: Tisha delivered a full React Server Components migration for the growth-experiments dashboard (PR #2104), introducing streaming so the page progressively renders rather than blocking on the slowest data fetch. Cold-load TTI dropped from 2.4s to 0.9s at P75 without breaking any downstream consumer APIs. She also extended the shared design-token system (PR #2178) to achieve dark-mode parity across 18 funnel components, replacing hardcoded colors with semantic tokens. This combination of performance engineering and design-system stewardship shows depth across the modern React stack.
evidence: PR #2104 — Delivered React Server Components migration with streaming and measured TTI improvement without breaking consumer APIs.
PR #2178 — Extended shared design tokens and achieved dark-mode parity for 18 components via token-driven theming.

skills.name: Web Security (CSP, SRI, XSS mitigation)
skills.summary: Strict CSP rollout across 5 surfaces with nonce allowlists and automated SRI hash-drift detection.
skills.description: Tisha implemented a strict Content Security Policy with nonce-based allowlists across 5 user-facing web surfaces (PR #2245), using a three-phase rollout — report-only, enforce on internal, enforce on production — with telemetry dashboards tracking violation rates at each stage. She added Subresource Integrity hashes for all 11 third-party scripts (PR #2261) and built an automated weekly cron that detects hash drift when a CDN-served script changes without a version bump. This work eliminated inline-script XSS vectors and established ongoing supply-chain monitoring — turning a one-time audit into continuous assurance.
evidence: PR #2245 — Implemented strict CSP with nonce-based allowlists, per-request nonce context, and a three-phase rollout with telemetry dashboards.
PR #2261 — Added Subresource Integrity hashes for 11 third-party scripts and automated weekly hash-drift detection.

skills.name: Authentication & Authorization hardening
skills.summary: Cookie hardening, CSRF token rotation, and a Q1 XSS audit across 47 surfaces fixing 6 risky patterns.
skills.description: PR #2289 hardened auth cookie attributes by adding __Host- prefix and SameSite=Strict, and implemented CSRF token rotation with a 30-second grace window that avoids breaking in-flight requests during rotation. The Q1 frontend XSS audit (SEC-201) reviewed 47 user-input surfaces, fixed 6 risky patterns including unsanitized innerHTML and reflected query params, and produced a regression checklist now used in frontend PR reviews. Together these close pen-test findings and raise the default security posture for all web surfaces.
evidence: PR #2289 — Hardened auth cookie attributes (__Host- prefix, SameSite) and implemented CSRF token rotation with a 30s grace window.
JIRA SEC-201 — Owned Q1 frontend XSS audit across 47 surfaces, fixed 6 risky patterns, and published a regression checklist.

skills.name: Database Migrations & Schema design
skills.summary: Cross-team schema migration with online pg_repack and zero-downtime coordination.
skills.description: Tisha performed a cross-team schema migration for the payments/fraud platform (PR #1389) using online pg_repack to reclaim bloat and reorganize indexes without downtime, coordinating with Rayan's team on timing and rollback plans. While database work isn't her primary domain, this contribution demonstrates the ability to operate safely on critical shared infrastructure when the need arises.
evidence: PR #1389 — Performed cross-team schema migration (payments/fraud platform) with online pg_repack in coordination with another engineer.

skills.name: Technical writing & mentorship (security runbooks, reviews)
skills.summary: Security review coordination, runbooks for CSP adoption, and direct pairing with other teams.
skills.description: Tisha coordinated cross-team security reviews through #sec-review, wrote runbooks for CSP adoption and nonce-pattern implementation, and provided direct pairing and mentorship on secure refactoring patterns. This work reduced the security team's review backlog by enabling other frontend teams to self-serve on common hardening patterns rather than waiting for a centralized review. The mentorship shows up in other teams' improved security posture, not just in Tisha's own commits.
evidence: Slack #sec-review (2026-03-25) — Coordinated cross-team security reviews, wrote runbooks, and provided direct pairing/mentorship on refactors and CSP adoption.

projectThemes.title: Frontend Modernization & Design Systems
projectThemes.summary: Migrated a legacy dashboard to React Server Components and expanded token-driven dark-mode parity across 18 components.
projectThemes.description: Tisha led the migration of the growth-experiments dashboard from a client-rendered React SPA to React Server Components with streaming (PR #2104), reducing cold-load TTI from 2.4s to 0.9s at P75 without breaking any downstream consumer APIs. She then extended the shared design-token system (PR #2178) to achieve full dark-mode parity across 18 funnel components, replacing hardcoded color values with semantic tokens so future theme changes propagate automatically. The streaming approach was chosen specifically to keep the dashboard usable during slow data fetches — the skeleton fills in progressively rather than blocking on the slowest query. This work unblocked the onboarding team, who had been waiting on dark-mode support before shipping their new merchant flow.

projectThemes.title: Security Hardening & Trust
projectThemes.summary: Strict CSP, Subresource Integrity, cookie hardening, and XSS audits closing pen-test findings across 5 web surfaces.
projectThemes.description: Tisha implemented a strict Content Security Policy with nonce-based allowlists across 5 user-facing web surfaces (PR #2245), using a three-phase rollout — report-only, enforce on internal, enforce on production — with telemetry dashboards tracking violation rates at each stage. She added Subresource Integrity hashes for all 11 third-party scripts (PR #2261) and built an automated weekly hash-drift detection cron that alerts when a CDN-served script changes without a corresponding version bump. Cookie hardening (PR #2289) introduced __Host- prefixed session cookies with SameSite=Strict and a CSRF token rotation scheme using a 30-second grace window to avoid breaking in-flight requests during rotation. She also owned the Q1 frontend XSS audit (SEC-201), reviewing 47 user-input surfaces, fixing 6 risky patterns (unsanitized innerHTML, reflected query params), and publishing a regression checklist now used in frontend PR reviews.

projectThemes.title: Cross-team Support & Technical Leadership
projectThemes.summary: Schema migration support for payments/fraud and security review coordination with runbooks and mentorship.
projectThemes.description: Tisha performed a cross-team schema migration (PR #1389) for the payments/fraud platform, running an online pg_repack to reclaim bloat and reorganize indexes with zero downtime in coordination with Rayan's team. She also took ownership of coordinating cross-team security reviews through #sec-review, writing runbooks for CSP adoption and nonce-pattern implementation that other frontend teams could follow independently. In multiple Slack threads she paired directly with engineers on safe refactoring patterns — walking them through how to eliminate inline scripts, adopt nonce contexts, and validate SRI hashes in their build pipelines. This mentorship work reduced the security team's review backlog by giving other teams the tools to self-serve on common hardening patterns.

contributionEvidence.summary: github: Migrates growth-experiments client to React Server Components + streaming; reduces dashboard TTI 2.4s → 0.9s (cold P75) without breaking consumer APIs.
github: Delivers strict CSP rollout with nonce-based allowlist, per-request nonce context, and a three-phase enforcement strategy; eliminates inline-script XSS vectors across 5 web surfaces.
github: Adds Subresource Integrity (SRI) hashes to all third-party script loads and a weekly hash-drift cron to detect CDN changes without runtime cost.
jira: Owns end-to-end Q1 frontend XSS audit across 47 user-input surfaces; fixes 6 risky patterns and publishes a regression checklist.
jira: Drives cookie hardening (__Host- prefix, SameSite) and CSRF token rotation with a 30s grace window to close pen-test findings.
slack: Performs cross-team security reviews (SEC-228 coordination) and mentors peers on CSP, nonce patterns, and safe refactors via #frontend-security and #sec-review.

summary: Arnav owns deep, multi-layered Postgres infrastructure work on the data-infra team — from diagnosing query planner regressions to leading a multi-quarter Postgres 16 fleet upgrade. His artifacts show a consistent pattern of building systems that balance correctness and performance (staleness-aware replica routing, replication backlog handling) and of making that knowledge accessible to others through RFCs, runbooks, and consistency-model documentation cited across multiple design reviews.

skills.name: Postgres Internals
skills.summary: Deep query planner diagnostics, fleet-wide version upgrades, and WAL sender behavior under load.
skills.description: Arnav's Postgres expertise spans the full stack from planner internals to replication protocol behavior. He root-caused a query planner regression on partitioned tables (DATAINFRA-104) to stale histogram statistics after a bulk load — the planner was choosing sequential scans instead of partition pruning — and fixed it with targeted ANALYZE scheduling and a planner hint. He owns the multi-quarter PG 14→16 upgrade (DATAINFRA-78) including extension compatibility audits for pgvector, pg_partman, and pg_repack, plus per-tenant validation. PR #3287 shows systems-level depth: diagnosing WAL sender stalling under long-running transactions and implementing a backlog-aware throttle with a parallel apply path that respects relation-level ordering.
evidence: DATAINFRA-104 — root-caused a query planner regression on partitioned tables to stale histogram statistics after a bulk load and resolved it with targeted ANALYZE scheduling and a planner hint.
DATAINFRA-78 — owns the multi-quarter PG 14→16 upgrade epic including extension compatibility audit (pgvector, pg_partman, pg_repack) and per-tenant validation.
PR #3287 — diagnosed WAL sender stalling behavior under long-running transactions and implemented a backlog-aware throttle with a parallel apply path that respects relation-level ordering.

skills.name: Distributed Systems / Replication
skills.summary: Staleness-aware replica routing and backlog-safe logical replication under 4× peak write load.
skills.description: Two substantial PRs demonstrate Arnav's ability to reason about distributed consistency and failure modes. PR #3210 implemented staleness-aware read-replica routing with per-query consistency annotations, allowing queries that tolerate eventual consistency to hit the nearest replica while strong-consistency queries route to the primary — cutting cross-AZ read latency by ~38% for the analytics dashboard. PR #3287 tackled the opposite problem: preventing logical replication from falling unrecoverably behind under 4× peak write load through producer-side throttling and a parallel apply path on the consumer side. DATAINFRA-78 designed a logical-replication-driven cutover strategy for the fleet-wide Postgres upgrade, showing he can apply replication knowledge to operational problems too.
evidence: PR #3210 — implemented staleness-aware read-replica routing with per-query consistency annotations, reducing cross-AZ read latency by ~38% for the analytics dashboard service.
PR #3287 — built producer-side throttling and a consumer parallel apply path to prevent logical replication lag from becoming unrecoverable under 4× peak write load.
DATAINFRA-78 — designed a logical-replication-driven cutover strategy for fleet-wide Postgres version upgrades.

skills.name: Technical Writing & Documentation
skills.summary: Cross-region consistency model cited in 4 design reviews, plus upgrade RFC and operational runbooks.
skills.description: Arnav authored the cross-region read consistency model document (DATAINFRA-141) defining the contract other teams build against: eventual consistency within 200ms at p99 by default, with opt-in strong consistency via primary routing. This doc was cited in four design reviews this quarter by teams outside data-infra. He also wrote the Postgres 16 upgrade RFC and operational runbook (DATAINFRA-78), the single reference for cutover sequence, rollback plan, and extension compatibility. These aren't write-and-forget docs — they're living coordination tools that reduce the bus factor on complex infrastructure decisions.
evidence: DATAINFRA-141 — authored the cross-region read consistency model doc (eventual within 200ms p99, opt-in strong consistency via primary routing), cited in 4 design reviews this quarter.
DATAINFRA-78 — wrote the upgrade RFC and operational runbook for the Postgres 16 fleet migration.

skills.name: System Ownership & Long-running Project Management
skills.summary: Sole owner of a multi-quarter, cross-fleet Postgres upgrade spanning extension audits and cutover strategy.
skills.description: DATAINFRA-78 is a multi-quarter project requiring sustained attention across extension compatibility audits, a logical-replication cutover strategy, and per-tenant validation — the kind of work where momentum dies if ownership isn't clear. Arnav self-reported and was assigned as sole owner, maintaining the RFC, tracking blockers, and driving per-tenant validation milestones. This is less about a specific technical skill and more about the ability to hold a long-horizon project together across sprints without losing context or urgency.
evidence: DATAINFRA-78 — self-reported and assigned as sole owner of a multi-quarter, cross-fleet Postgres upgrade covering extension audits, cutover strategy, and per-tenant validation.

skills.name: Performance Investigation & Query Optimization
skills.summary: Root-cause analysis of planner regressions and measured 38% cross-AZ latency reduction.
skills.description: Arnav independently traced a slow query reported by the analytics team (DATAINFRA-104) to a partition pruning failure caused by stale planner statistics after a bulk load, then shipped a targeted ANALYZE + planner hint fix in the same sprint. PR #3210 achieved a ~38% reduction in cross-AZ read latency through replica routing changes, with the improvement measured and validated against production query profiles. Both cases show a disciplined approach: measure first, diagnose the specific bottleneck, fix it, then validate the outcome — not speculative tuning.
evidence: DATAINFRA-104 — independently traced an analytics team-reported slow query to partition pruning failure caused by stale planner statistics and shipped a targeted fix.
PR #3210 — measured and achieved a ~38% reduction in cross-AZ read latency through replica routing changes.

projectThemes.title: Postgres Fleet Operations & Upgrades
projectThemes.summary: Multi-quarter Postgres 14→16 fleet upgrade and query planner regression fix on partitioned tables.
projectThemes.description: Arnav owns the multi-quarter Postgres 14→16 upgrade across the entire data-infra fleet (DATAINFRA-78), a project spanning extension compatibility audits (pgvector, pg_partman, pg_repack), a logical-replication-based cutover strategy that avoids downtime windows, and per-tenant validation to catch schema drift before promotion. He wrote the upgrade RFC and operational runbook that SRE and dependent teams reference for their own cutover planning. Separately, when the analytics team reported a sudden slow-query regression on partitioned tables (DATAINFRA-104), Arnav traced the root cause to stale histogram statistics after a bulk load — the planner was choosing sequential scans instead of partition pruning. He shipped a targeted fix combining scheduled ANALYZE runs with a planner hint, restoring query performance within the same sprint.

projectThemes.title: Read Scalability & Replication Reliability
projectThemes.summary: Staleness-aware replica routing cutting cross-AZ latency 38% and backlog-aware replication throttling.
projectThemes.description: PR #3210 introduced staleness-aware read-replica routing with per-query consistency annotations — queries that tolerate eventual consistency are routed to the nearest replica, while strong-consistency queries go to the primary. This reduced cross-AZ read latency by approximately 38% for the analytics dashboard service without sacrificing correctness guarantees. PR #3287 addressed a different failure mode: under 4× peak write load, the logical replication consumer was falling behind faster than it could apply changes, eventually hitting an unrecoverable lag state. Arnav added producer-side backlog-aware throttling that slows the write path when the consumer falls behind a configurable threshold, combined with a parallel apply path that respects relation-level ordering constraints while processing independent table changes concurrently. Together these two changes transformed the replication layer from a fragile bottleneck into a system that degrades gracefully under load.

projectThemes.title: Knowledge Sharing & Cross-team Support
projectThemes.summary: Cross-region consistency model doc cited in 4 design reviews, plus upgrade RFC and runbooks.
projectThemes.description: Arnav authored the cross-region read consistency model document (DATAINFRA-141) that defines the contract other teams build against: eventual consistency within 200ms at p99 by default, with opt-in strong consistency via primary routing for use cases that need it. This document has been cited in four design reviews this quarter by teams outside data-infra, including the fraud-platform team's payment-router redesign and the growth team's experiment-results pipeline. He also wrote the Postgres 16 upgrade RFC and operational runbook (DATAINFRA-78), which serves as the single reference for the cutover sequence, rollback plan, and extension compatibility matrix. By producing these shared artifacts, Arnav reduced the bus factor on complex infrastructure decisions and gave other teams a foundation for making sound architectural choices without needing to consult his team directly.

contributionEvidence.summary: PR #3210 — implemented staleness-aware read-replica routing with per-query consistency annotations, cutting cross-AZ read latency ~38% for the analytics dashboard service.
PR #3287 — added producer-side backlog throttling and a parallel apply path on the consumer to prevent Postgres logical replication lag from becoming unrecoverable under 4× peak write load.

DATAINFRA-78 — owns and authored the RFC and runbook for the multi-quarter Postgres 14→16 upgrade across the data-infra fleet, including extension compatibility and logical-replication-driven cutover.
DATAINFRA-104 — root-caused a query planner regression on partitioned tables to stale histogram statistics and shipped a targeted ANALYZE + planner hint fix for the analytics team.
DATAINFRA-141 — authored the cross-region read consistency model document (eventual/200ms p99 with opt-in strong consistency), cited in 4 design reviews this quarter.

summary: Sahib has built and shipped foundational internal tooling and observability infrastructure that other teams depend on daily. Their work spans developer experience automation, CI efficiency, and distributed tracing — consistently oriented toward reducing friction for the broader engineering org. Recent artifacts also show early moves into technical leadership, taking ownership of a cross-team review process.

skills.name: Developer Experience Tooling
skills.summary: Zero-to-service CLI adopted by 5 teams, cutting setup from ~2 days to ~30 minutes.
skills.description: Sahib authored platform-cli scaffold (PR #512), a CLI tool that codifies the company's golden-path service template: TypeScript, OpenTelemetry instrumentation, CI/CD pipeline, Helm chart, and an on-call stub. Before this tool, spinning up a new service required roughly two days of manual scaffolding. Within two weeks of launch, five teams had adopted it, and the announcement in #platform-eng surfaced immediate cross-team feature requests including a fraud-platform variant. This is high-leverage tooling that compounds: every new service now starts from a consistent, instrumented baseline.
evidence: PR #512 — authored platform-cli scaffold, a zero-to-service CLI that reduced new service setup from ~2 days to ~30 min, adopted by 5 teams within two weeks of launch.
Slack thread 2026-01-23 — coordinated launch in #platform-eng, fielded cross-team feedback from david and rayan, and triaged a follow-up request to support fraud-platform's service.yaml shape.

skills.name: CI/CD Pipeline Engineering
skills.summary: Parallelized monorepo CI and added lockfile-keyed caching — wall time cut from 18 to 7 minutes.
skills.description: PR #538 restructured the monorepo CI pipeline by splitting the serial test run into parallel stages grouped by package and introducing a node_modules cache keyed on the lockfile hash. This more than halved CI wall time from 18 to 7 minutes. Sahib also removed a redundant lint job duplicating pre-commit checks, eliminating a source of false-positive failures. He took ongoing ownership by announcing the improvement in #internal-tooling and proactively asking teams to report flakiness — not just shipping and walking away.
evidence: PR #538 — parallelized test stages by package and introduced a lockfile-keyed node_modules cache, cutting monorepo CI wall time from 18 min to 7 min.
Slack thread 2026-02-14 — announced the CI improvement in #internal-tooling and proactively asked for flakiness reports, indicating ongoing ownership of the pipeline.

skills.name: Observability & Distributed Tracing (OpenTelemetry)
skills.summary: Replaced home-grown tracing with OpenTelemetry across the API gateway and 23 downstream services.
skills.description: PR #489 replaced a home-grown tracing layer with OpenTelemetry across the shared API gateway, implementing W3C trace-context propagation, per-route sampling config, and a tail-based sampler capturing 100% of slow requests. The migration was validated against the fraud-scoring path's latency budget in coordination with Rayan — confirming zero p99 impact. Follow-up PRs migrated 23 downstream services off the old SDK, eliminating trace-break bugs at service boundaries. The result is unified request tracing from ingress through every internal hop.
evidence: PR #489 — replaced a home-grown tracing layer with OpenTelemetry across the shared API gateway, adding context propagation, per-route sampling config, and a tail-based sampler for slow requests.
PR #489 description — coordinated with rayan to verify zero latency impact on the fraud-scoring path, then migrated 23 downstream services off the old SDK in follow-up PRs.

skills.name: Cross-team Coordination & Adoption
skills.summary: Drove adoption of tooling across 5+ teams and coordinated a 23-service migration.
skills.description: Both the OTel migration (PR #489, 23 downstream services) and platform-cli (PR #512, 5 teams adopting in 2 weeks) required Sahib to drive adoption across teams he doesn't manage. The OTel work needed buy-in from the fraud-platform team for latency budget validation. Platform-cli adoption required handling feature requests from teams with different service.yaml shapes. This pattern of building something, getting it adopted, and then maintaining it across organizational boundaries is a core coordination skill.
evidence: PR #489 — migration touched 23 downstream services and required buy-in from at least the fraud-platform team (rayan's latency budget review).
PR #512 and Slack 2026-01-23 — platform-cli was adopted by 5 teams within two weeks, with Sahib handling feature requests from teams outside platform-experience.

skills.name: Technical Leadership (emerging)
skills.summary: Nominated to lead 2 ICs and own the service-template review rubric.
skills.description: Sahib's engineering manager nominated him to lead a small group of two ICs and own the evaluation rubric for the new service-template review process (Slack #eng-mentorship, 2026-03-09). This is an early-stage leadership signal built on the domain expertise from platform-cli and golden-path template work. Sahib accepted and framed it as aligned with his growth goal of leading a small team. The rubric he's building will standardize how the org evaluates whether new services meet baseline requirements for observability, CI, and operational readiness.
evidence: Slack thread 2026-03-09 — eng-manager nominated Sahib to lead a small group of 2 ICs and own the rubric for a new service-template review process; Sahib accepted and expressed intent to grow in this direction.

projectThemes.title: Internal Developer Experience & Automation
projectThemes.summary: Built platform-cli scaffold, cutting new service setup from ~2 days to ~30 minutes — adopted by 5 teams.
projectThemes.description: Sahib authored platform-cli scaffold (PR #512), a zero-to-service CLI that codifies the company's golden-path service template: TypeScript project structure, OpenTelemetry instrumentation, CI/CD pipeline, Helm chart, and an on-call stub with PagerDuty wiring. Before this tool, spinning up a new service required roughly two days of manual scaffolding and config copying; the CLI reduced that to about 30 minutes. Within two weeks of launch, five teams had adopted it, and the announcement in #platform-eng (2026-01-23) immediately surfaced cross-team feature requests — including a request from the fraud-platform team for a variant supporting their service.yaml shape. The tool's adoption rate signals genuine org-wide leverage: every new service now starts from a consistent, instrumented baseline rather than a copy-paste of whatever the last team happened to ship.

projectThemes.title: Observability Infrastructure Migration
projectThemes.summary: Replaced home-grown tracing with OpenTelemetry across the API gateway and 23 downstream services.
projectThemes.description: Sahib led the migration of the shared API gateway from a home-grown tracing layer to OpenTelemetry (PR #489), implementing W3C trace-context propagation, per-route sampling configuration, and a tail-based sampler that captures 100% of slow requests (>500ms) while sampling normal traffic at 5%. The migration was validated against the fraud-scoring path's latency budget in coordination with Rayan's team — confirming zero additional p99 latency from the new instrumentation. After the gateway migration landed, Sahib followed up with PRs migrating 23 downstream services off the old tracing SDK, eliminating a class of context-propagation bugs where traces would break at service boundaries. The result is a unified tracing layer where any on-call engineer can follow a request from ingress through every internal hop to the database — something that previously required stitching together logs from three different systems.

projectThemes.title: CI Efficiency & Build Infrastructure
projectThemes.summary: Parallelized monorepo CI and added lockfile-keyed caching, cutting wall time from 18 to 7 minutes.
projectThemes.description: Sahib restructured the monorepo CI pipeline (PR #538) by splitting the single serial test run into parallel stages grouped by package, and introducing a node_modules cache keyed on the lockfile hash so dependency installation only runs when dependencies actually change. This more than halved CI wall time from 18 minutes to 7 minutes. He also identified and removed a redundant lint job that duplicated checks already enforced by the pre-commit hook, shaving another minute and eliminating a source of false-positive failures. After shipping, he announced the improvement in #internal-tooling (2026-02-14) and proactively asked teams to report any new flakiness — taking ongoing ownership of pipeline health rather than treating it as a one-shot optimization.

projectThemes.title: Early Technical Leadership
projectThemes.summary: Nominated to lead 2 ICs and own the service-template review rubric — first formal leadership scope.
projectThemes.description: Sahib's engineering manager nominated him to lead a small group of two ICs and own the evaluation rubric for the new service-template review process (Slack #eng-mentorship, 2026-03-09). The assignment recognizes the domain expertise Sahib built through platform-cli and the golden-path template work — he's the natural owner of what 'good' looks like for new services. While this is an early-stage leadership signal rather than a fully established track record, Sahib accepted the role and explicitly framed it as aligned with his growth goal of leading a small team. The rubric he's building will standardize how the org evaluates whether new services meet baseline requirements for observability, CI, and operational readiness.

contributionEvidence.summary: PR #489 — Migrated the shared API gateway from a home-grown tracer to OpenTelemetry with context propagation and tail-based sampling; subsequently migrated 23 downstream services off the old SDK.
PR #512 — Built platform-cli scaffold, reducing new service setup from ~2 days to ~30 min; adopted by 5 teams within two weeks of launch.
PR #538 — Parallelized monorepo CI test stages and added a lockfile-keyed node_modules cache, cutting average wall time from 18 min to 7 min.
Slack #platform-eng 2026-01-23 — Announced platform-cli launch, fielded feedback from multiple teams, and triaged a follow-up request to add a fraud-platform service template variant.
Slack #internal-tooling 2026-02-14 — Communicated the CI speedup to the broader org and took ownership of any resulting pipeline flakiness.
Slack #eng-mentorship 2026-03-09 — Accepted a technical leadership assignment to lead 2 ICs and own the service-template review rubric.

summary: Priya's artifacts show a track record of high-impact infrastructure work on the ML platform — migrating embedding storage to pgvector for a 12x p99 latency improvement and building an internal LLM eval harness used across teams. She consistently works across team boundaries, providing technical review and guidance beyond her immediate scope in data-infra.

skills.name: Vector Search & Embedding Infrastructure
skills.summary: Faiss-to-pgvector migration delivering 12x p99 latency improvement and $11k/month cost savings.
skills.description: Priya migrated the recommendation system's embedding store from a custom Faiss-on-disk sharded layout to pgvector with HNSW indexing (PR #4072, 28 files changed). Nearest-neighbor query latency dropped from 480ms to 38ms at p99 — a 12x improvement — while also gaining transactional consistency on embedding updates that the Faiss setup couldn't provide. She confirmed the production deployment in #ml-platform (2025-12-11) including $11k/month cost savings from eliminating dedicated index hosts. This is end-to-end infrastructure ownership: design, implementation, migration, cost analysis, and production validation.
evidence: PR #4072 — migrated recsys embedding store from Faiss-on-disk to pgvector with HNSW indexes, cutting p99 nearest-neighbor latency from 480ms to 38ms across 28 changed files.
Slack #ml-platform 2025-12-11 — announced live migration with $11k/mo cost savings on bespoke index hosts, confirming end-to-end ownership through deployment.

skills.name: LLM Evaluation & Model Quality Tooling
skills.summary: Internal eval harness with RAG accuracy, instruction-following, and refusal-calibration scoring across providers.
skills.description: PR #4159 is a 1,100+ line internal eval harness that provides deterministic, statistically rigorous comparison of LLM providers through the AI Gateway. It covers three dimensions — RAG retrieval accuracy, instruction-following fidelity, and refusal calibration — outputting side-by-side JSON reports with confidence intervals and significance bands. Any team shipping an LLM feature can run calibrated regression scores without custom integration work. Cross-team adoption has already started: a colleague in #ai-tooling-curious (2026-03-04) requested a walkthrough, and Priya proactively offered onboarding.
evidence: PR #4159 — authored a 1,100+ line internal eval harness covering RAG accuracy, instruction-following, and refusal-calibration with statistical significance bands across AI Gateway providers.
Slack #ai-tooling-curious 2026-03-04 — proactively offered to walk a colleague through the eval harness, indicating knowledge transfer and cross-team adoption of the tool.

skills.name: ML Systems Operability & Cost Reduction
skills.summary: Eliminated bespoke Faiss infrastructure, saving $11k/month with zero new operational overhead.
skills.description: The pgvector migration (PR #4072) wasn't just a latency win — it eliminated the bespoke Faiss shard infrastructure entirely, saving approximately $11k/month in dedicated index hosting costs. The nightly HNSW index rebuild was designed to fit within the existing database maintenance window, meaning zero new operational overhead for the on-call team. This reflects a deliberate approach to infrastructure decisions: optimizing for operational simplicity alongside raw performance.
evidence: PR #4072 — eliminated bespoke Faiss shard infrastructure in favor of Postgres-native pgvector, reducing operational footprint and saving ~$11k/month.
PR #4072 description notes nightly index build fits the existing maintenance window, demonstrating deliberate planning around operational constraints.

skills.name: Cross-team Technical Review
skills.summary: Identified a gap in the fraud team's shadow-compare methodology and recommended tail latency tracking.
skills.description: In #payments-fraud (2025-09-22), Priya reviewed the fraud team's Stripe migration performance gate design and identified a specific gap: the team was comparing mean latencies between old and new payment paths but wasn't tracking tail latency divergence. A regression affecting only the slowest 1% of requests could have slipped through undetected. She recommended adding p99 divergence tracking, and Rayan's team incorporated it into the gate criteria. This cross-team review — deep systems intuition catching a blind spot in another domain — is high-value invisible work.
evidence: Slack #payments-fraud 2025-09-22 — reviewed the Stripe migration perf gate design for the fraud team, recommending tail latency divergence tracking between shadow paths, which was adopted by the reviewer (rayan).

skills.name: Statistical & Experimental Rigor
skills.summary: Eval harness uses statistical significance bands and confidence intervals, not just raw accuracy scores.
skills.description: The eval harness (PR #4159) outputs side-by-side JSON reports with statistical significance bands, enabling teams to distinguish real quality differences from noise when comparing LLM providers. This goes beyond simple accuracy metrics — it applies evaluation methodology rigor (confidence intervals, significance testing) to ensure teams make model selection decisions on solid statistical footing rather than vibes or single-run comparisons.
evidence: PR #4159 — eval harness outputs side-by-side JSON reports with statistical significance bands, indicating applied knowledge of evaluation methodology beyond simple accuracy metrics.

projectThemes.title: Recommendation Infrastructure Modernization
projectThemes.summary: Migrated embedding store from Faiss to pgvector — 12x p99 latency improvement and $11k/month cost savings.
projectThemes.description: Priya led the end-to-end migration of the recommendation system's embedding store from a custom Faiss-on-disk sharded layout to pgvector with HNSW indexing (PR #4072, 28 files changed). The migration delivered a 12x p99 latency improvement — nearest-neighbor queries dropped from 480ms to 38ms — while also providing transactional consistency on embedding updates that the Faiss setup lacked. By moving to Postgres-native vector search, the team eliminated the bespoke Faiss shard infrastructure entirely, saving approximately $11k/month in dedicated index hosting costs. The nightly HNSW index rebuild was designed to fit within the existing database maintenance window, avoiding any new operational overhead. She confirmed the production deployment outcomes in #ml-platform (2025-12-11), demonstrating full ownership from design through production validation.

projectThemes.title: LLM Evaluation Tooling
projectThemes.summary: Built an internal LLM eval harness with statistical significance bands, now adopted across multiple teams.
projectThemes.description: Priya designed and shipped an internal eval harness (PR #4159, 1,100+ lines) that provides deterministic, statistically rigorous comparison of LLM providers through the AI Gateway. The harness covers three evaluation dimensions — RAG retrieval accuracy, instruction-following fidelity, and refusal calibration — outputting side-by-side JSON reports with confidence intervals and statistical significance bands so teams can distinguish real quality differences from noise. The tool routes through the company's AI Gateway, meaning any team shipping an LLM-powered feature (search, summarization, fraud explanations) can run calibrated regression scores against multiple providers without custom integration work. Adoption has already extended beyond data-infra, with a colleague in #ai-tooling-curious (2026-03-04) requesting a walkthrough — and Priya proactively offered to onboard them, seeding cross-team knowledge transfer.

projectThemes.title: Cross-team Infrastructure Support & Review
projectThemes.summary: Reviewed the fraud team's Stripe migration perf gate and identified a gap in their shadow-compare methodology.
projectThemes.description: Priya contributed unsolicited but high-value technical guidance outside her immediate team by reviewing the payments-fraud team's Stripe migration performance gate design in #payments-fraud (2025-09-22). She identified a specific gap in the shadow-compare methodology: the team was comparing mean latencies between the old and new payment paths, but wasn't tracking tail latency divergence — meaning a regression that only affected the slowest 1% of requests could slip through undetected. She recommended adding p99 divergence tracking between the shadow paths, and the fraud-platform team (Rayan) incorporated the suggestion into the gate criteria before the migration went live. This kind of cross-team review — where deep systems intuition from one domain catches a blind spot in another — is exactly what internal discovery is meant to surface.

contributionEvidence.summary: PR #4072 — replaced bespoke Faiss-on-disk embedding store with pgvector + HNSW, achieving a 12x p99 latency reduction (480ms → 38ms) and ~$11k/month cost savings.
PR #4159 — built an internal LLM eval harness with deterministic scoring (RAG accuracy, instruction-following, refusal-calibration) and statistical significance bands across AI Gateway providers.
#ml-platform 2025-12-11 — confirmed live deployment of pgvector migration including cost and latency outcomes, demonstrating full ownership through production.
#payments-fraud 2025-09-22 — provided unsolicited but adopted cross-team feedback on tail latency divergence tracking for the Stripe migration perf gate.
#ai-tooling-curious 2026-03-04 — offered to onboard colleagues onto the eval harness, surfacing cross-team knowledge-sharing beyond data-infra.

summary: Jonas is a staff engineer whose artifacts center on org-wide incident-response infrastructure and system reliability. He reduced P1 MTTR from 47 to 19 minutes across the org in Q4 2025, established structured postmortem practices adopted by seven teams, and consistently makes invisible maintenance and ownership work visible for the engineers around him.

skills.name: Incident Response & MTTR Reduction
skills.summary: Drove org-wide P1 MTTR from 47 to 19 minutes with structural incident-response changes.
skills.description: Jonas led the Q4 incident-response improvement epic (SRE-201), implementing three structural changes: formalized IC rotation ensuring a named incident commander within 3 minutes of any P1, a shared runbook template standardizing the first 10 minutes of response, and a mandatory 48-hour post-P1 paging review that catches alert-fatigue drift. MTTR dropped from 47 to 19 minutes with zero missed pages during holiday peak. The INC-2026-0202 postmortem confirmed the auto-paging chain delivered human eyes in 4 minutes — validating these changes hold under real incident conditions.
evidence: SRE-201 — drove org-wide P1 MTTR from 47 min to 19 min in Q4 2025 by standardizing incident-commander rotation, shared runbook templates, and auto-paging review cadence.
Slack #incidents 2026-02-02 — closed postmortem for INC-2026-0202, confirmed auto-paging chain delivered human eyes in 4 minutes, and assigned dated action items for runbook refresh (SRE-289).

skills.name: Runbook Authoring & Maintenance
skills.summary: Shared runbook templates, quarterly refresh process, and pre-cutover drill discipline.
skills.description: Jonas introduced a shared runbook template as part of the Q4 incident-response epic (SRE-201), giving every team a consistent format for documenting first-response procedures. During the INC-2026-0202 postmortem, he identified a 6-month stale runbook and filed SRE-289 to establish a quarterly refresh cadence — turning a one-off discovery into a systematic process. Before the Stripe cutover (#sre 2025-11-04), he confirmed the rollback runbook was current and completed a kill-switch drill as primary on-call. This pattern — write the runbook, drill it, then build a process to keep it fresh — shows operational maturity beyond just firefighting.
evidence: SRE-201 — introduced a shared runbook template as one of three structural changes in the Q4 incident-response improvement epic.
Slack #incidents 2026-02-02 — identified a 6-month stale runbook during postmortem review and filed SRE-289 to establish a quarterly refresh process.
Slack #sre 2025-11-04 — confirmed rollback runbook was prepared and kill-switch drill completed before the Stripe cutover, serving as primary on-call.

skills.name: Postmortem Facilitation & Technical Writing
skills.summary: Structured postmortem template adopted by all 7 SRE-aligned teams, replacing ad-hoc formats.
skills.description: SRE-244 produced a structured postmortem template that enforces timeline with timestamps, contributing factors (not 'root cause'), bounded customer impact, a 'what went well' section, and time-boxed action items with named owners and due dates. All seven SRE-aligned teams adopted it in Q1 2026. Jonas applied it himself during INC-2026-0202, demonstrating the template works in practice — not just in theory. The emphasis on contributing factors over root-cause blame and on what went well alongside what broke reflects a mature understanding of organizational learning.
evidence: SRE-244 — authored a structured org-wide postmortem template (timeline, contributing factors, customer impact, owner-assigned action items, 'what went well') that replaced ad-hoc formats and was adopted by all 7 SRE-aligned teams in Q1.
Slack #incidents 2026-02-02 — executed the new template in practice: updated timeline, bounded customer impact, and assigned due-dated action items for INC-2026-0202.

skills.name: Cross-team On-call & Reliability Support
skills.summary: Primary on-call for cross-team Stripe cutover and SRE support for fraud-scoring through Q4 peak.
skills.description: Jonas took primary on-call for the Stripe payment-system cutover (#sre 2025-11-04), a cross-team event outside his normal fraud-platform scope, pairing a shadow engineer for coverage continuity. For FRAUD-44, he provided SRE-side on-call coverage, runbook reviews, and kill-switch drills to support the fraud-scoring system through Q4 holiday traffic. This willingness to extend reliability support beyond team boundaries — especially for high-stakes events — reduces organizational single-point-of-failure risk.
evidence: Slack #sre 2025-11-04 — took primary on-call for the Stripe payment-system cutover, a cross-team event outside fraud-platform, with a shadow engineer paired for coverage.
FRAUD-44 — provided SRE-side on-call coverage, runbook reviews, and kill-switch drills to support the fraud-scoring system through Q4 holiday traffic.

skills.name: Making Invisible Work Visible (Advocacy & Sponsorship)
skills.summary: Filed a Jira epic to document a colleague's 18-month solo system ownership ahead of promotion review.
skills.description: Jonas explicitly filed FRAUD-44 to surface Rayan's 18 months of solo fraud-scoring system maintenance work ahead of a promotion cycle, ensuring the contribution was documented and credited. This is peer advocacy using organizational tools: recognizing that long-running maintenance work is routinely undercounted in promotion processes and taking concrete action to make it legible to decision-makers. It's the kind of sponsorship behavior that senior engineers can provide and that directly embodies OrgGraph's mission of making invisible work visible.
evidence: FRAUD-44 — explicitly filed a Jira epic to surface Rayan's 18 months of solo fraud-scoring system maintenance work ahead of a promotion cycle, ensuring the contribution was documented and credited.

projectThemes.title: Incident Response Infrastructure
projectThemes.summary: Cut org-wide P1 MTTR from 47 to 19 minutes through structural incident-response improvements.
projectThemes.description: Jonas led a quarter-long effort (SRE-201) to systematically reduce org-wide P1 incident response time through three structural changes: formalizing the incident-commander rotation so every P1 has a named IC within 3 minutes, authoring a shared runbook template that standardizes the first 10 minutes of any incident, and establishing a mandatory 48-hour post-P1 paging review that catches alert-fatigue drift before it compounds. The result was P1 MTTR dropping from 47 minutes to 19 minutes with zero missed pages during the Q4 holiday traffic peak. During the Stripe payment-system cutover (#sre 2025-11-04), Jonas served as primary on-call for a cross-team event outside his normal fraud-platform scope, completing a kill-switch drill beforehand and pairing a shadow engineer for coverage continuity. The live postmortem for INC-2026-0202 (#incidents 2026-02-02) confirmed the auto-paging chain delivered human eyes in 4 minutes — validating the structural changes held under real incident conditions.

projectThemes.title: Postmortem Practice & Organizational Learning
projectThemes.summary: Designed a structured postmortem template adopted by all 7 SRE-aligned teams, replacing ad-hoc formats.
projectThemes.description: Jonas designed and deployed a structured postmortem template (SRE-244) that replaced the previous ad-hoc formats used inconsistently across the org. The template enforces a specific structure: timeline with timestamps, contributing factors (explicitly not 'root cause' — avoiding single-cause blame), bounded customer impact quantification, a 'what went well' section to reinforce good practices, and time-boxed action items with named owners and due dates. All seven SRE-aligned teams adopted the template in Q1 2026. Jonas applied it himself during the INC-2026-0202 postmortem (#incidents 2026-02-02), updating the timeline in real time, bounding customer impact to a specific blast radius, and assigning dated action items including SRE-289 — a quarterly runbook refresh process he filed after discovering a 6-month stale runbook during the review. The template has become the default format for all post-incident reviews.

projectThemes.title: System Ownership Visibility & Peer Advocacy
projectThemes.summary: Filed an epic to make a colleague's 18-month solo system ownership visible ahead of a promotion cycle.
projectThemes.description: Recognizing that long-running maintenance work is routinely undercounted in promotion processes, Jonas filed FRAUD-44 explicitly to document Rayan's 18 months of solo ownership of the fraud-scoring system — the kind of invisible, unglamorous work that keeps critical infrastructure running but rarely shows up in sprint demos or feature announcements. The epic captured the scope of that ownership: on-call coverage, performance tuning, incident response, and the operational knowledge that only exists in one person's head. Jonas also provided direct SRE support — on-call coverage during high-traffic periods, runbook reviews, and kill-switch drills — to reduce the single-person risk on the system while the documentation effort was underway. This is a concrete example of peer advocacy: using organizational tools (Jira, formal documentation) to make invisible work legible to the people who make promotion decisions.

contributionEvidence.summary: SRE-201 — led Q4 org-wide incident-response improvement epic, cutting P1 MTTR from 47 min to 19 min with three structural changes and zero missed pages during holiday peak.
SRE-244 — authored structured postmortem template adopted by all 7 SRE-aligned teams in Q1 2026, replacing ad-hoc formats with a blameless, owner-assigned framework.
FRAUD-44 — filed epic to make a colleague's 18-month solo system-maintenance contribution visible before a promotion cycle, while providing direct SRE on-call support.
#incidents 2026-02-02 — ran live postmortem for INC-2026-0202, confirmed paging chain health, flagged stale runbook, and filed SRE-289 for quarterly refresh cadence.
#sre 2025-11-04 — served as primary on-call for cross-team Stripe cutover, completed kill-switch drill, and paired a shadow engineer for coverage continuity.

summary: Mei's artifacts show a frontend engineer who digs into concrete performance problems and builds product features end-to-end. In under a year she shipped a custom windowing solution that raised scroll FPS from 22 to 60 and cut memory by ~40 MB, and she built a resilient multi-step merchant onboarding flow that handles a real upstream cold-start failure. She actively seeks mentorship and cross-team knowledge to deepen her mental model of frontend performance at scale.

skills.name: React Performance Optimization
skills.summary: Custom IntersectionObserver windowing that raised scroll FPS from 22 to 60 and cut memory by 40 MB.
skills.description: Mei profiled the experiments-list page on an M1 MacBook Air, measured 22 FPS during scroll and excessive memory from react-window's full-DOM approach, then replaced it with a custom IntersectionObserver-backed windowing implementation (PR #2230) that only mounts rows entering the viewport. Scroll FPS jumped to a stable 60 and heap memory dropped ~40 MB. The PR includes explicit before/after benchmarks on target hardware — this was measured, iterated, and validated, not a speculative swap. She's also pairing with a senior IC to build deeper performance mental models, making this a deliberate skill trajectory.
evidence: PR #2230 — replaced react-window with a custom IntersectionObserver-backed windowing impl, raising scroll FPS 22→60 and cutting memory ~40 MB on the experiments-list page.
PR #2230 description explicitly benchmarks M1 Air scroll FPS and heap memory delta, showing non-trivial measurement and iteration, not just a library swap.

skills.name: Complex Form & Multi-step UI Authoring
skills.summary: Full merchant onboarding flow with multi-step state, optimistic submission, and retry logic.
skills.description: PR #2271 (22 files, 980 additions) is the complete frontend for the new merchant onboarding experience: a multi-step form with state persistence across steps, optimistic submission for responsiveness, and retry logic for upstream failures. The scope — form architecture, error boundaries, API integration, and UX polish — demonstrates the ability to own a feature end-to-end from design handoff through production, not just implement isolated components.
evidence: PR #2271 — built the full frontend for the new merchant onboarding flow: multi-step form, optimistic submission, and retry logic (22 files changed, 980 additions).

skills.name: Resilient API Integration (retry/error handling)
skills.summary: Diagnosed upstream cold-start 502s and implemented retry-with-backoff in the submission flow.
skills.description: When intermittent 502s appeared during onboarding submission, Mei surfaced the issue in #frontend-help (2025-11-22) and correctly scoped it to the fraud-scoring service's cold-start behavior — avoiding what could have been a longer frontend investigation. She then implemented retry-on-502 with 200ms backoff directly in the submission flow (PR #2271) with documented rationale. The combination of diagnosis and fix in the same sprint shows practical resilience engineering: understanding failure modes across the stack, not just the frontend.
evidence: PR #2271 — implemented retry-on-502 with 200 ms backoff after diagnosing the fraud-scoring service cold-start issue surfaced in #frontend-help on 2025-11-22.
Slack thread #frontend-help 2025-11-22 — identified that intermittent 502s were an upstream cold-start, not a frontend bug, and applied the fix correctly.

skills.name: Cross-functional Debugging & Problem Diagnosis
skills.summary: Correctly scoped an ambiguous 502 to an upstream service cold-start, avoiding a longer frontend investigation.
skills.description: In #frontend-help (2025-11-22), Mei surfaced intermittent 502 errors that could have been attributed to a frontend bug. She investigated across the stack and correctly identified the fraud-scoring service's cold-start behavior as the cause, then integrated the fix (retry with backoff) into production code with documented rationale from Rayan confirming the diagnosis. This cross-functional debugging instinct — knowing when a frontend symptom has a backend cause — is valuable and often underrecognized.
evidence: Slack thread #frontend-help 2025-11-22 — surfaced and correctly scoped an ambiguous 502 error to the fraud-scoring service, avoiding a longer frontend investigation.
PR #2271 — integrated the diagnosis directly into production code with documented rationale (Rayan's note).

skills.name: Design Systems Awareness & Adoption
skills.summary: Actively tracks design system releases and connects them to unblocked product work.
skills.description: In #design-systems (2026-02-19), Mei acknowledged that dark-mode parity shipped by Tisha unblocked a week of work on the onboarding flow. This isn't a deep technical skill signal, but it shows active tracking of cross-team design system outputs and the ability to connect infrastructure changes to product delivery timelines — awareness that helps teams coordinate without explicit dependency management.
evidence: Slack thread #design-systems 2026-02-19 — acknowledged dark-mode parity shipped by Tisha unblocked a week of work on the onboarding flow, indicating active tracking and use of design system outputs.

projectThemes.title: Frontend Performance Engineering
projectThemes.summary: Custom windowing solution raising scroll FPS from 22 to 60 and cutting memory by ~40 MB.
projectThemes.description: Mei identified a real-world scroll performance regression on the experiments-list page — a high-row-count table where users reported visible jank. She profiled the page on an M1 MacBook Air, measuring 22 FPS during scroll and excessive memory allocation from react-window's full-DOM-node approach. Rather than tweaking the library's configuration, she replaced it with a custom IntersectionObserver-backed windowing implementation (PR #2230) that only mounts rows entering the viewport. The result: scroll FPS jumped from 22 to a stable 60, and heap memory dropped by approximately 40 MB. The PR includes explicit before/after benchmarks on the target hardware, showing this wasn't a speculative optimization — it was measured, iterated, and validated. She's also actively pairing with a senior frontend IC (#eng-mentorship 2026-03-12) to build deeper mental models of rendering performance at scale, indicating this is a deliberate skill investment rather than a one-off fix.

projectThemes.title: Merchant Onboarding Flow
projectThemes.summary: Full frontend build for merchant onboarding — multi-step form, optimistic submission, and upstream cold-start resilience.
projectThemes.description: Mei owned the complete frontend build for the new merchant onboarding experience (PR #2271, 22 files changed, 980 additions): a multi-step form with state persistence across steps, optimistic submission for a responsive UX, and retry logic with 200ms backoff for upstream 502 errors. The retry logic came from her own debugging — she surfaced intermittent 502s in #frontend-help (2025-11-22) and correctly scoped the issue to the fraud-scoring service's cold-start behavior, avoiding what could have been a longer frontend investigation. Rayan from the fraud team confirmed the diagnosis, and Mei integrated the fix directly into the submission flow with documented rationale. The work was also unblocked by Tisha's dark-mode design token parity (#design-systems 2026-02-19), which Mei was actively tracking — demonstrating awareness of cross-team dependencies and the design system's release cadence.

projectThemes.title: Mentorship & Professional Development Seeking
projectThemes.summary: Proactively requested recurring pairing with a senior IC, targeting performance mental models at scale.
projectThemes.description: Mei proactively requested a recurring pairing slot with a senior frontend IC in #eng-mentorship (2026-03-12), framing a specific and actionable learning goal: building mental models for frontend performance at scale — understanding not just how to profile a slow page, but how to predict which architectural patterns will cause performance problems before they ship. This is self-directed growth behavior: she identified a gap in her own expertise based on the windowing work she'd just completed, and sought structured mentorship rather than waiting for a manager to assign it. The pairing arrangement indicates the senior IC saw enough signal in Mei's existing work to invest time, and the specificity of the ask (performance at scale, not generic frontend mentorship) suggests Mei has a clear picture of where she wants to go next.

contributionEvidence.summary: PR #2230 — custom windowing + IntersectionObserver replaced react-window; scroll FPS 22→60, memory −40 MB on experiments-list page.
PR #2271 — full frontend for merchant onboarding: multi-step form, optimistic submission, and 502 retry-backoff for fraud-scoring cold-start (22 files, 980 additions).
#frontend-help 2025-11-22 — surfaced intermittent 502s on fraud-scoring API, correctly scoped issue to upstream cold-start, applied fix in same sprint.
#eng-mentorship 2026-03-12 — proactively requested regular pairing with senior frontend IC, citing a specific learning goal around performance at scale.
#design-systems 2026-02-19 — tracked dark-mode parity shipped by Tisha and immediately connected it to unblocking the upcoming onboarding flow work.

summary: David's artifacts show early but substantive contributions spanning backend refactoring and developer-experience tooling. His work demonstrates an ability to take on non-trivial system changes — coordinating design with senior engineers before writing code — and a pragmatic instinct for reducing friction in CI/review workflows. He is actively building exposure in infra-adjacent areas and has begun seeking mentorship toward a platform/infra path.

skills.name: Async programming and concurrency patterns
skills.summary: Sync-to-async refactor of fraud rules-engine with bounded-queue backpressure across 18 files.
skills.description: PR #1322 refactored the fraud rules-engine evaluation path from synchronous to async execution, adding a bounded queue for backpressure that ensures the pipeline degrades gracefully under load rather than consuming unbounded memory. The change spanned 18 files (+642/-401 lines) and replaced blocking I/O calls with async equivalents throughout. David proactively sought design review from Rayan (#eng-mentorship, 2026-02-08) before writing code, validating the error-handling approach to avoid introducing race conditions in rule evaluation ordering. This design-first approach on a cross-team codebase shows deliberate thinking about async complexity.
evidence: PR #1322 — refactored the fraud rules-engine evaluation path from synchronous to async, adding a bounded queue for backpressure across 18 files (+642/-401 lines).
Slack #eng-mentorship 2026-02-08 — proactively sought design review from Rayan on error-handling before writing code, showing deliberate approach to async complexity.

skills.name: CI/CD tooling and pipeline development

projectThemes.title: Backend Systems & Async Refactoring
projectThemes.summary: Refactored fraud rules-engine evaluation from sync to async with bounded-queue backpressure across 18 files.
projectThemes.description: David's most substantial engineering work is the refactor of the fraud rules-engine evaluation path from synchronous to asynchronous execution (PR #1322, 18 files changed, +642/-401 lines). The change introduced a bounded queue for backpressure — ensuring the evaluation pipeline degrades gracefully under load rather than consuming unbounded memory — and replaced blocking I/O calls with async equivalents throughout the rules-evaluation chain. Before writing code, David proactively sought design review from Rayan (#eng-mentorship, 2026-02-08), a senior engineer on the fraud-platform team, to validate the error-handling approach and ensure the async refactor wouldn't introduce subtle race conditions in rule evaluation ordering. This design-first approach on a cross-team codebase he doesn't own day-to-day demonstrates careful judgment about system correctness and willingness to work outside his comfort zone.

projectThemes.title: Developer Experience & CI Tooling
projectThemes.summary: Automated Helm chart diff previews on PRs, eliminating manual helm template runs during code review.
projectThemes.description: David shipped a targeted DX improvement to the shared CI pipeline (PR #545): automated Helm chart diff previews posted as PR comments whenever a service.yaml file is touched. Before this tool, reviewers had to manually run helm template locally to see the rendered diff — a step that was frequently skipped, leading to config bugs discovered only at deploy time. The implementation detects service.yaml changes in the PR, runs helm template against the base and head commits, and posts a formatted diff comment with the rendered values. Sahib from the platform-cli team immediately integrated it into recommended workflows (#internal-tooling, 2026-03-04), and David framed it within the broader platform-cli ecosystem rather than as a standalone tool — showing awareness of how CI tooling composes. The tool addresses a concrete, recurring pain point that every team deploying via Helm encounters.

projectThemes.title: Infra-Adjacent Growth & Community Engagement
projectThemes.summary: Actively pursuing infra knowledge — participating in #infra-curious and framing recent work as steps toward infra engineering.
projectThemes.description: David has begun deliberately pursuing infrastructure knowledge beyond his current platform-experience team scope, as evidenced by his participation in #infra-curious (2026-03-15) where he publicly signaled his intent to transition toward an infra engineering path and sought mentorship from engineers who have successfully made the product-to-infra transition. He's framing his recent work — Helm chart tooling (PR #545), the fraud-platform async refactor (PR #1322), and CI pipeline contributions — as deliberate steps in that direction rather than incidental cross-team help. This is a career signal rather than a demonstrated skill cluster at this stage, but it provides important context for understanding the trajectory of David's contributions: he's building toward infra systems work intentionally, not just responding to ad-hoc requests.
